title: If We Reach the Stars
subtitle: The Forgotten Question at the Heart of Humanity’s Greatest Adventure
description: The Forgotten Question at the Heart of Humanity’s Greatest Adventure
body_html:
There is something in the human person that has never been fully at home here.

Not in the anxious, escapist sense, not the dissociation of someone fleeing their life, but something older and stranger. A constitutional restlessness that seems woven into the fabric of what we are. Augustine called it the heart that finds no rest until it rests in God. The desert fathers called it penthos, a holy ache. The physicists and the tech visionaries are calling it something different now. They are calling it the stars.

The question underneath all the orbital launch schedules and humanoid robots and longevity research is the same question it has always been: what kind of creature are we, and what are we for?

The Vision

The case being made by the people building this future is genuinely staggering. Within thirty years, they believe, humanoid robots will outnumber humans on Earth by a factor of ten. AI will have solved most human disease. The cost of surgery will approach the cost of electricity. As Peter Diamandis recently put it, “all of the things I would get to someday — someday is right now.”

The centerpiece of this acceleration is space. Orbital data centers. Rotating colonies — vast cylinders spinning in the dark, simulating Earth gravity, housing thousands of people at a time. A permanent, irreversible expansion of human civilization beyond the planet. Elon Musk has predicted ten billion humanoid robots on Earth by 2040. Demis Hassabis of DeepMind has said publicly that he believes we will cure all disease within the next ten years. The promise of longevity escape velocity — the point at which science extends your life faster than you age — may arrive in this decade.

There is beauty in this. I want to say that plainly, because the reflexive Christian response to techno-optimism is usually suspicion, and sometimes that suspicion is earned, but not always. The cosmos is not alien to the sacred. The Psalms erupt with it — the heavens declaring glory, the stars as handiwork, the depths of the universe as testimony to a Creator who is not made small by the scale of what He made. When Bonaventure wrote that creation helps the human mind “ascend gradually to the supreme Principle, who is God,” he was not speaking metaphorically. The cosmos is not a backdrop. It is a disclosure.

The mandate in Genesis — be fruitful and multiply, fill the earth and subdue it — has always been misread as license for domination. But the Hebrew carries the weight of stewardship, of a creature entrusted with something precious, called to extend and cultivate and care for what was given. If the earth is not the limit of creation, it is not obviously strange that the human mandate of cultivation might eventually press outward. The Church has consistently held that humans are called to develop their abilities in science and technology, for through them, God is glorified. That is not a footnote. It is a theological claim about the dignity of human creativity.

So the question is not simply whether we should go. It is who we will be when we get there.

The question is not simply whether we should go to the stars. It is who we will be when we get there.
